titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Brewer Family Papers
titlestmt.author: compiled by Katharine Morton

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Tuesday August 11, 2020 at 07:32
Finding aid revision description not supplied.

did.unittitle: Brewer family papers
did.abstract: The papers consist of correspondence, writings, speeches, scrapbooks, photographs, diaries, and other papers, primarily of David Josiah Brewer, lawyer and justice of the U.S. Supreme Court. The material includes Brewer's personal and family papers, although there are some papers relating to public matters, notably the Anglo-Venezuelan boundary dispute. Also included are papers of five generations of Brewer family members, including Josiah Brewer (1796-1872), the father of David Josiah Brewer. Nearly all of the family material consists of correspondence and is largely related to family matters.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The materials are open for research.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Brewer Family Papers (MS 99). Manuscripts and Archives, Yale University Library.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Gifts of David Brewer Karrick, 1951; David B. Hall, 1957; and Mrs. Charles H. Parker, 1965.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged in two series: I. David Josiah Brewer Papers. II. Brewer Family Papers.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Brewer Family Papers consist primarily of the papers of David Josiah Brewer, Justice of the Supreme Court, although papers of other members of the Brewer family are also included. The collection has been arranged into two series: DAVID JOSIAH BREWER and BREWER FAMILY. The former consists of the correspondence, writings, scrapbooks, photographs, and personal papers of Justice Brewer. These materials provide a wealth of biographical information about David Josiah Brewer. They are not, however, particularly helpful in tracing the development of Brewer's ideas as a jurist; rather, they provide insight into his character, his family life, and his role in public life as a well-known lecturer on national and international affairs. (The bulk of his Public Papers are in the Library of Congress.)

The correspondence of David Josiah Brewer includes his business and legal correspondence, as well as correspondence with family and friends. Many of the letters are related to Brewer's frequent speaking engagements, to publishing and editing ventures, to investments, or to family business matters. Some of the correspondence is related to specific legal cases.

In Brewer's correspondence are letters from Supreme Court Justices Peckham, Brown, and Harlan, the latter writing in 1893 from the Bering Sea Arbitration Court in Paris. Other correspondents represented in the collection are: Timothy Dwight of Yale; Henry Wade Rogers, Dean of the Yale Law School and a President of Northwestern University; Judge James C. Jenkins, Dean of the Marquette University Law School; William F. Warren, President of Boston University; H.J. Dennis, state librarian of Kansas (re: Republican and Populist confrontations in the Kansas state legislature in 1892); William M. Randolph, a Memphis lawyer (re: Tennessee politics during the Hayes Administration); and Maria Longworth Storer (re: President Theodore Roosevelt's removal of her husband Bellamy as ambassador to Austria-Hungary). Unfortunately, there are only one or two letters from each of the individuals mentioned above. Brewer's correspondence with family members is, in general, more substantial. The two major correspondents in this category are Brewer's first wife, Louise Landon Brewer (twenty-seven letters) and his daughter, Henrietta Brewer Karrick (approximately seventy letters and telegrams from David Josiah Brewer).

One small segment of correspondence is made up of letters of David Josiah Brewer and others pertaining to the Anglo-Venezuelan boundary dispute. The twelve letters, which are grouped together, date from 1896 to 1904. Among the correspondents are: Clements R. Markham, president of the Royal Geographic Society of London; Lord Salisbury, of the Foreign Office; Severo Mallet-Prevost, secretary to the Venezuelan Frontier Committee; and John M. Hay, Secretary of State.

The writings of David Josiah Brewer are composed primarily of addresses and published articles. There are also, however, letters to the editor, a few judicial decisions and instructions to juries, poems, and notes. Among Justice Brewer's notes is a typescript of the treatise on international law written for the Cyclopedia of Law and Procedure (1906) by David Josiah Brewer and Charles Henry Butler. The typescript contains corrections and editing in longhand by Brewer. Many writings of David Josiah Brewer are to be found in the three scrapbooks kept by Brewer himself from 1856 until his death in 1910.

Brewer's addresses, both manuscript and published, were delivered during the years 1865 to 1910. There are approximately eighty addresses in the collection. The articles, nearly fifty in number, date from the late 1860's to 1910. Brewer's articles and addresses cover a wide range of topics, most of which are still relevant today. Discussing international affairs, he spoke about the dangers of imperialism ("The Spanish War, a Prophecy or an Exception?"), the need for arbitration of international disputes and the limitation of armaments (addresses at the Lake Mohonk Conferences on International Arbitration). On other occasions, Brewer spoke to national needs and problems. The growth of big corporations, the use of the injunction, the misuse of public office for private gain, a new role for women in American society, and the education of blacks in the U.S. were some of his topics. As one might expect, the courts and the legal profession provided material for a number of articles and addresses. The Supreme Court, legal ethics, the right of appeal (which Brewer proposed to eliminate) and the education of lawyers were among his favorite subjects. As a judge, he was very concerned with the problems of individual liberty and the idea of the State. A life-long Congregationalist, David J. Brewer often addressed church groups and wrote for religious publications on Christian life and the future of the church. He spoke of service to others at meetings of organizations like the Y.M.C.A. and the American Missionary Association; he spoke of public service at colleges and universities such as Yale.

In addition to "Correspondence" and "Writings," Series I also contains "Special Files," consisting of biographical material and memorabilia, financial records, and photographs. Here one will find three scrapbooks of David Josiah Brewer, which cover the years 1856 to 1910, a scrapbook of Louise Landon Brewer (1881-1898), and three scrapbooks (1901-1910) kept by Emma Mott Brewer, Justice Brewer's second wife. The scrapbooks, especially those kept by Brewer himself, are an excellent source of biographical information. There are clippings and articles written about Brewer during his lifetime, as well as sketches of his life published with memorial tributes. Other items included in "Special Files" are Brewer's diplomas, honorary degrees, certificates, and a large number of invitations, programs, and souvenirs (principally of social events in Washington from 1890 to 1910). The financial records of David Josiah Brewer include both personal and business papers. There are contracts, bonds, receipts, and an account book listing family expenses and legal clients. A few financial papers pertaining to legal cases are also found here. Among the photographs in this series are portraits of David Josiah Brewer and other members of the U.S. Supreme Court, as well as photographs belonging to Brewer.

The "Subjects File" of Series I contains copies of many official documents of the Anglo-Venezuelan Boundary Commission, including reports, historical and geographical evidence presented by Great Britain, Venezuela, and impartial experts, memoranda issued by both sides, and a copy of the treaty of arbitration signed by the two countries in 1897. Another portion of the "Subjects File" is composed of writings of others on international law and arbitration collected by Justice Brewer. Finally, there is a small amount of clippings, statistics and memorabilia pertaining to the U.S. Supreme Court.

Series II, BREWER FAMILY, consists of the correspondence, writings, diaries, personal papers, clippings and photographs of five generations of Brewers, from the early nineteenth to the mid-twentieth century.

Josiah Brewer (1796-1872) is the only family member represented by a sizable amount of material. The father of David Josiah Brewer and a missionary to Smyrna from 1827 to 1838, Josiah Brewer established several schools for girls in Asia Minor. Upon his return to the U.S., he worked as a prison chaplain in Wethersfield, Conn. (1839-1842), as editor of the Union Missionary Herald and other religious publications (1842-1843), as director of the Elm Street Female Seminary in New Haven (1850-1857). Pastor of a church near Stockbridge, Mass., from 1857 to 1866, Josiah Brewer died in Stockbridge in 1872. His correspondence contains letters written to him from nineteenth century educators and clergymen throughout the U.S. and from American missionaries in all parts of the world. There are also six of his sermons and a biographical sketch.

The bulk of the correspondence contained in Series II is between members of the Brewer family. One exception is the correspondence of Mrs. Emma Mott Brewer, which includes letters from people in Washington society and condolences on the death of her husband, David Josiah Brewer. Another exception is the correspondence of Mrs. Frances Landon Woods, the sister-in-law of David Josiah Brewer, which includes two letters of Benjamin Harrison and two of Chief Justice Melvin Fuller. Of special interest are copies of letters written during the Civil War by Lt. Marshall Bidwell Brewer, the brother of David Josiah Brewer, to members of his family in the North. During the summer of 1862, when the letters were written, Marshall Brewer was stationed at Camp Belger, Baltimore.

There are few writings within the series. Diaries were kept by Frances Landon Woods in Kansas City, Missouri, during the years 1880 and 1885, and intermittently by Frances Adele Brewer, daughter of David Josiah Brewer, during the years 1890 to 1895. There is an article on Tarsus written in 1904 by Elizabeth Hale Brewer, a missionary and teacher in Greece.

Three other collections are closely related to the Brewer Family Papers: the Bidwell Family Papers, the Field Family Papers, and the David Brewer Karrick Papers. The Bidwells and the Brewers intermarried on two occasions: Theodosia Bidwell (1766-1841) married Eliab Brewer (1770-1804), the grandfather of David Josiah Brewer; and Henrietta Whitney Brewer (1831-1901), the sister of David, married Lawson Bennett Bidwell (1833-1922). The Field and Brewer families are related through David J. Brewer's mother, Emilia Ann Field (1807-1861). The papers of David Brewer Karrick, the grandson of David J. Brewer, contain the correspondence of Henrietta Brewer Karrick and her husband, James Lawson Karrick, as well as many letters from members of the Brewer family.
Additional information on the Brewer family genealogy can be foundhere.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: David Josiah Brewer was born in Smyrna, Asia Minor, on June 20, 1837. He was the son of Rev. Josiah Brewer, a missionary to Turkey who established several schools for girls in Smyrna and neighboring regions. David J. Brewer's mother was Emilia Ann Field, a sister of Supreme Court Justice Stephen J. Field and of Cyrus W. Field, who laid the first Atlantic cable.

Upon their return to the United States in 1838, the Brewer family settled in Wethersfield, Connecticut, where Josiah Brewer was chaplain of the state prison. He held subsequent positions as editor, teacher and pastor, and moved with his family from Hartford, to New Haven, to Middletown, Connecticut, and finally to Stockbridge, Massachusetts.

While in Middletown, David J. Brewer entered Wesleyan College at the age of fourteen. After two years, he transferred to Yale, his father's Alma Mater. In 1856, he graduated from Yale with honors. Brewer studied law at the office of his uncle, David Dudley Field, and at the Albany Law School, from which he received a Bachelor of Laws Degree in 1858.

Deciding to go West, the young lawyer established a practice in Leavenworth, Kansas, where he married Louise Landon in 1861. That same year Brewer was appointed commissioner of the federal circuit court for the district of Kansas. He began his judicial career in 1862, when he was elected judge of the probate and criminal courts of Leavenworth County. Three years later, in 1865, Brewer became judge of the first judicial district of Kansas, a position he held until 1869, when he assumed the post of city attorney of Leavenworth. Elected a justice of the Supreme Court of Kansas in 1870, at the age of thirty-three, Brewer served for fourteen years, winning re-elections in 1876 and 1882.

President Arthur appointed Brewer to the federal circuit court for the eighth circuit in 1884. Five years later, he received an appointment from President Harrison to succeed Stanley Matthews on the U.S. Supreme Court. Brewer assumed his duties as associate justice in 1890 and served for twenty years until his death in 1910.
One of Brewer's colleagues on the bench of the U.S. Supreme Court was his uncle, Stephen J. Field, who served from 1863 to 1898. Another, Justice Henry B. Brown, was a classmate of Brewer's at Yale.

Brewer was president of the commission created in 1895 to settle the boundary dispute between British Guiana and Venezuela. In 1898 the matter was settled by arbitration. His work on the commission deepened his concern for international peace and reinforced his belief in arbitration as a peaceful means of settling international disputes. Justice Brewer participated in most of the Lake Mohonk Conferences on International Arbitration and was a vice-president of the American Society of International Law.

The son of a missionary, Brewer was actively involved in a number of charitable organizations, such as the American Missionary Association, the American Bible Society, and the Associated Charities of Washington, D.C. He shared his father's concern for the rehabilitation of prisoners, especially during his years in Leavenworth.

While in Washington, D.C., Justice Brewer was a member of the faculty of the Columbian Law School (now the law school of George Washington University). In 1902 he delivered the Dodge Lectures at Yale on American citizenship and was in constant demand as a speaker, lecturing extensively across the country.

A trustee of Northwestern Mutual Life Insurance Company and a founder of the Intercontinental Correspondence University in Washington, D.C., Justice Brewer also found time to edit two collections entitled World's Best Essays and World's Best Orations.
In 1896 one of his four daughters died, followed by his wife in 1898. Emma Miner Mott became Brewer's second wife in 1901. Brewer himself died at his home in Washington on March 28, 1910.
For a chart outlining the genealogical relations of the Brewer family, please consult theGenealogical Chart.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: Unpublished materials authored or otherwise produced by the creator(s) of this collection are in the public domain. There are no restrictions on use. Copyright status for other collection materials is unknown. Transmission or reproduction of materials protected by U.S. Copyright Law (Title 17, U.S.C.) beyond that allowed by fair use requires the written permission of the copyright owners. Works not in the public domain cannot be commercially exploited without permission of the copyright owners. Responsibility for any use rests exclusively with the user.

did.unittitle: David Josiah Brewer (1837-1910)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series is divided into four sub-sections:

"Correspondence" contains letters to and from David Josiah Brewer, grouped together and arranged alphabetically by the correspondent's last name. It also contains correspondence of others and correspondence about the Anglo-Venezuelan boundary dispute, each of which is arranged chronologically.

"Writings, Notes, and Memoranda" of David J. Brewer consists of his addresses, articles, arranged chronologically, followed by notes, and memoranda, also arranged chronologically. (The three scrapbooks of David Josiah Brewer, in "Special Files," contain many of his writings which do not appear in this subsection.)
For details on the scrapbooks, see Appendix I.

In "Special Files" may be found biographical materials, memorabilia, financial records, and photographs. Biographical materials and memorabilia, as well as financial records, are arranged chronologically. The photographs, for the most part undated, are simply listed by folder title.
There are three categories in the "Subjects File": Anglo-Venezuelan Boundary Commission, International Law, and the U.S. Supreme Court. The materials in each category are in chronological order.
The number of items is contained in parentheses before unitdates.

altformavail.head: Existence and Location of Copies
altformavail.p: A copy of this material is available in digital form from Manuscripts and Archives. Contact Manuscripts and Archives at mssa.assist@yale.edu to request access to the digital copy.

odd.head: General note
odd.p: See: Appendix I for writings of D. J. Brewer (addresses, articles, letters to the editor, poems) appearing in his scrapbooks but not appearing in this section. See also: Orbis for additional addresses & articles of D. J. Brewer Note: Folders 124 & 125 are empty but filed in Box 3.

did.unittitle: Writings of D. J. Brewer (addresses, articles, letters to the editor, poems) appearing in his scrapbooks but not appearing in this section. See also: Appendix 1

odd.head: General
odd.p: Newspaper clippings about articles and addresses of D.J. Brewer. See: Series I. folder 269-270

did.unittitle: Programs on speaking engagements of D.J.B.

odd.head: General
odd.p: Programs of speaking engagements of D.J.B. See: Series I, folder 240

did.unittitle: Notes, Memos, and Poems:
Notes: speaking engagements
Memos: Supreme Court
Memo: the Pious Fund of California & resultant claims, settled
Typescript of treatise on international law written by D.J.B. & Charles Henry Butler for the Cyclopedia of Law and Procedure (1906) (with corrections by Brewer)
Typescript on international law: claims of citizens against foreign states
Typescript on international law: settlement of disputes, arbitration, Hague conferences (with corrections)
Typescript on international law: extradition (with corrections)
Typescript on international law: neutral governments (with corrections)
Poem to his grandmother, Submit Dickinson, at a Field family gathering
Poems to his wife, Louise (Lulu) Landon Brewer
Poem on the 50th wedding anniversary of Cyrus W. Field & Mary B. Stone
Poem on the U.S. vs. Wong Kim Ark
Two pages from the diary of D.J.B.
Special Files:
Biographical Material and Memorabilia:
Scrapbooks of David Josiah Brewer:

odd.head: General note
odd.p: For more details, see also: Appendix I

did.unittitle: No. I, containing selected poems, sermons, and newspaper clippings, as well as original writings
No. II, containing complete texts of his speeches, judicial decisions, letters to the editor, articles, and clippings of his career
No. III, containing complete texts of articles and addresses, published letters, interviews, & newspaper clippings of his career
Yale University, Class of 1856:
Senior prizes, clipping, class record of 1901 and 1906
Fraternity pins, lapel buttons, 50th reunion charm of 1906
Bachelor of Laws degree from the University of Albany
Certificate of admission as an attorney to the U.S. Disctict Court of Kansas
Certificate of election to the office of Judge of the Probate Court of Leavenworth County
Petition with 23 signatures asking D.J. Brewer to enter the election of the first Judicial District, Leavenworth, Kansas
Recommendation of D.J.B. for U.S. Circuit Court Justice, addressed to the President of the U.S. & signed by the Governor and 13 officials of the state of Kansas
Scrapbook of Mrs. D.J. Brewer (Louise R. Landon Brewer), includes clippings of her husband & family; invitations and responses
Certificate of membership in the Bar Association of Kansas.
Newspaper clippings: appointment of D.J.B. to the U.S. Circuit Court
Programs of speaking engagements of D.J.B.
Certificate of appointment to the U.S. Supreme Court, signed by President Harrison, with oath of office signed by D.J.B.
Farewell banquet to D.J.B.
Newspaper clippings: Brewer's appointment to the U.S. Supreme Court
Complimentary passes for railroads and expositions
Invitations, programs, menu, and Brewer family cards
Program of the Columbian Law School, 1891, and classbook, 1896 (D.J. Brewer, faculty)
Rules of the U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals for the Eighth Circuit. Adopted 1891 Jun 17, David J. Brewer, Circuit Justice
Newspaper clippings: descriptions of the living jurist
Certificate of life membership in the American Bible Society
National Armenian Relief Committee, total receipts for each year
Certificate of life membership in the Boston Seaman's Friend Society
Certificate of the American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
Plaque commemorating Justice Brewer's Presidency of the Venezuelan Boundary Commission
Northwestern Mutual Life Insurance Co. yearly statements for 1897 and 1906 (D.J.B., trustee)
"A Lawyer Who Worked His Way Up," by Henry Macfarland, The Christian Endeavor World
Invitations, menu & seating charts, in Paris
Invitation to the ceremonies of laying the cornerstone of the U.S. Government Building in Chicago
" World's Best Orations , Comments and Criticisms," bound volume of newspaper clippings and letters
The World's Best Orations , letters of praise to the publisher
Catalogue of the School of Comparative Jurisprudence & Diplomacy, the Columbian University, Washington, D.C. (D.J.Brewer, faculty)
"Essay Reading for Delight," The American Lyceum Circle Series, No. 1 (includes photo of D.J.B.)
The Supreme Court of the United States , a series of biographical sketches of its members, edited by A. Feather (includes sketch of D.J. Brewer)
Honorary Doctor of Laws degrees: University of Wisconsin (1900), Wesleyan University (1901), and Bowdoin College (1905)
Scrapbooks of Mrs. D. J. Brewer (Emma Mott Brewer):
No. I, including clippings of her engagement & wedding, & invitations to Washington social events
No. II, including invitations and acceptances to Washington social events
No. III, including invitations & acceptances to Washington social events
Memorabilia of Pres. Wm. McKinley; souvenirs of second inauguration, John Hay's tribute to the late pres.
Advertisements for The World's Best Essays , D.J. Brewer, editor; F.P. Kaiser, publisher
Newspaper clippings: articles of D.J.B., reaction to
Newspaper clippings: accounts of addresses by David J. Brewer
Program of the International Congress of Arts & Sciences, Universal Exposition, St. Louis
Program of the Universal Congress of Lawyers & Jurists, Universal Exposition, St. Louis
Commemorative diploma of the St. Louis Exposition
Honorary membership in the Chattanooga Historical Society
List of living members of the Class of 1855, Wesleyan University
Nominations to the Hall of Fame, report to the board of electors
Prospectus, constitution & officers of the American Society of International Law, D.J. Brewer, vice president
Stock prospectus of the Intercontinental Correspondence University of Washington, D.C.; D.J. Brewer, a founder

altformavail.head: Existence and Location of Copies
altformavail.p: A copy of this material is available in digital form from Manuscripts and Archives. Contact Manuscripts and Archives at mssa.assist@yale.edu to request access to the digital copy.

Resolutions on the death of D.J. Brewer, Kansas City Bar Assn.

Newspaper clippings:

D.J.B. in Kansas
"25 Years Ago Today,"
Henrietta Wells Kennard receives deposit made for her by her grandfather, David J. Brewer
Little Red Schoolhouse between Stockbridge & South Lee, Mass., attended by D.J.B.
"The Education of a Justice. Justice Bradley and Some of His Colleagues," by Charles Fairman
Newspaper clipping: opening of Harlan-Brewer House at George Washington University
Addresses and memoranda
Advertisements for The World's Best Orations , D.J. Brewer, editor-in-chief, F. P. Kaiser, publisher
American Patriotic Reading Circle: application for enrollment; D.J.B. member of advisory board
Anecdotes about D.J.B.
Calling cards, principally of people in Washington society
Charlotte, Vt. (Thompson's Point), summer home of D.J.B., photographs and clippings
Clippings, misc.
Clippings: selected verses, misc.
"The first 300 names already approved by the advisory board in May" (?), includes name of D.J. Brewer
Notebook of minerals collected by D.J.B.
Order form for Modern Eloquence , autograph edition-de-luxe
Printed material, misc.
Regulations for the dining hall, Yale College
Financial Records:
Account book
Bill of costs, Cutts vs. Short , Court of the 1st Judicial District, Kansas, 1875 Feb 19; receipt of payment by D.J.B.
Account of family expenditures
Bond of D.J.B. to the Northwestern Mutual Life Insurance Co.
Receipt of payment by D.J.B., Woods' Real Estate and Exchange Agency
Copy of a letter to unknown recipient from George A. Eddy & D.J. Brewer accompanying appraisement & inventory of Mrs. Cutts' estate
Copy of policy of the Mutual Life Insurance Co. of New York for D.J.B.
Check for $100 from Mrs. E. D. Frank to D.J.B.
Agreement between D.J.B. and the Mackiernan Company
Contract for Telephone Service
Guardianship of Emily T. Dodge
Receipt for donation to the Associated Charities and the Citizens' Relief Assn.
Received of Henrietta Brewer Karrick by Fred Heis, payment for granite marker
List of property, list of stocks & bonds; owner unidentified

D.J. Brewer as a young man

Members of the Supreme Court

Subject File:
Anglo-Venezuelan Boundary Commission:
Correspondence re: the Anglo-Venezuelan Boundary Commission
Articles from the Treaty of Utrecht between Spain & the Netherlands
Report on British Guiana (1851), proceedings of the "Court of Policy" of Br. Guiana (copies)
Government notice of 1867 Jan 30 (copy), British Guiana re: British Guiana Gold Co.
"Extracts from History of the Colonies of Essequebo, Demerara & Berbice... " by Martinus Nyhoff
"Hartsinck, Description of Guyana, Amsterdam, 1770," translated & compiled by F. DeHaan of Johns Hopkins University
"A Report to the Venezuelan Commission on the Disputed Boundary between British Guiana and Venezuela," by Justin Winsor
Memorandum from Carácas, 1896 Mar 25, in response to a published letter of Lord Salisbury, dated 1895 Nov 26
"Expeditions of the Dutch to Guiana on the Wild Coast previous to the founding of the West Indian Company in 1621," translated by F. DeHaan
Memorandum of 1896 May 28 (copy) re: Hague Records and Justice Brewer's letter of 1896 May 6.
"The Contention About the Limitation of Boundary Between Venezuela and British Guiana Elucidated by General P.M. Netscher," reprint from the Tijdspiegel , 1896, translated by F. DeHaan
Newspaper clippings re: British Guiana-Venezuelan Boundary Commission
The Treaty of Arbitration Between Venezuela and Great Britain (copy)
Group photographs of the members of the Anglo-Venezuelan Arbitration Commission
List of official documents resulting from the Anglo-Venezuelan boundary dispute, compiled by Marcus Baker
"Along What Lines Should the Report of the Commission Run?"; "Papers to be Prepared,"
"Report upon the Cartographical Evidence of Geographers" by Severo Mallet-Prevost of Venezuela
"Sir Robert Schomburgk's Survey,"
Spanish Settlements on the Orioco, 7 pages of an unidentified report to the commission
International Law:
"On War and Peace," selections from the papers of Benjamin Franklin
"Message from the Pres. of the U.S. transmitting a copy of the convention for the pacific settlement of international disputes... and other new methods of a similar nature... signed at the Hague,"
Report of the Fifth Annual Meeting of the Lake Mohonk Conference on International Arbitration
"The Law of Prize," by Everett P. Wheeler, presented at the Rouen Conference
"No Protectorate, but an Alliance," by Dr. Paul Carus, in The Open Court
"The International Court of Arbitration at the Hague," by F. W. Holls, in The American Lawyer
"United States Doctrine of Citizenship and Expatriation," by Hon. Oscar S. Straus
A Code of International Law--Is It Possible? by T. S. Woolsey, in Yale Law Journal
A proclamation by the President of the U.S., Theodore Roosevelt (Neutrality Russia and Japan)
"The Work of the Second Hague Conference," by James Brown Scott
Program of New England Arbitration and Peace Conference (D. J. Brewer had been scheduled to speak)
"Future of Arbitration Among the Nations," by Baron d'Estrournelles de Constant
Notes of D. J. Brewer on international law and arbitration
Clipping: "The Pressing Armaments Question,"
Supreme Court:
U.S. Supreme Court: statistics on no. of cases filed
Church of the Holy Trinity vs. U.S. , opinion of the U.S. Supreme Court, delivered by Justice Brewer
Newspaper clippings
In Memoriam, Howell Edmunds Jackson . Proceedings of the U.S. Supreme Court in memory of Justice Jackson
Menu of dinner commemorating 25 years of service on the U.S. Supreme Court by John M. Harlan, with photo
U.S. Supreme Court passes
The law's delays, Supreme Court of N. Y., corrective measures
Photographs of Justices before and during Brewer's service.
Brewer Family

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series is made up of the correspondence, writings, diaries, clippings, and photographs of five generations of Brewer family members. The series is organized by individual family members, approximately twenty in number, listed alphabetically.

Correspondence is filed under the name of the family member to whom the letter was written, and by the name of the writer of the letter. Under a given family member's name is his or her correspondence, followed by writings, diaries, legal papers, clippings and photographs, in that order. Except for the correspondence, each of these sections is in chronological order. It is to be noted, however, that most family members will not have materials in every category mentioned. Consult Reference Archivist at repository for more information about the correspondents and genealogical chart in paper finding aid.

At the end of the series is a small group of materials (called "Brewer Family, General") which cannot be ascribed to one individual. Here, among other items, are group photographs and photographs of family friends.

Stone family
Non-family, sorted chronologically (3 folders)
Non-family, sorted chronologically (4 folders)
Diaries (7 folders)
Memorabilia from European travel
Notebooks and notes from Yale College lectures (also includes records of the Temperance Society)
Notebook
Notes on family history
Writings (7 folders)
Obituaries, memorial tributes
APPENDIX I
Writings of David Josiah Brewer which appear in Scrapbook I:
Articles, n.d.:
Something for the Dentists
A Sabbath in the Country, written for the Middlesex Republican
Evening at Nahant, written for the Middlesex Republican
The Tendency of Democratic Principles
Letters to the Editor of the Middlesex Republican , n.d.:
Franklin Pierce
The Dred Scott Case
Legislative Interference
Poem, n.d.: "Dred Scott," written for the Middlesex Republican
Writings of D.J.B. which appear in Scrapbook II and do not appear elsewhere in his papers:
Articles:
County Commissioners (contains the opinion of the County Attorney, David J. Brewer)
Notable Cases; Supreme Court Decisions (series of articles on the State Supreme Court, signed "B.," possibly D. J. Brewer)
The Supreme Court
The Law of Descent (signed "B.," possibly D.J.Brewer)
Two Features of the Proposed Amendment (article on the temperance issue, signed "B.," possibly D.J.Brewer)
Increase of Litigation - Its Causes and Cure (written for the Homestead)
Constitutional Convention
Untitled article on the Sabbath
Justice Brewer Interviewed, Yale Daily News
A Kansas Citizen (a tribute to the late George A. Eddy), n.d.
Complete Texts of Addresses - In Scrapbook II (Box 7, Folder 228):
The New Profession (delivered to the State Normal School)
Address on scientific research to the State Teachers' Association at Manhattan
Address on education (condensed)
Address at the dedication of the New Normal Building
Address on libel
Address on the danger to the family and the home, delivered to the New England society
Instructions to the jury by Judge Brewer, Frankie Morris vs. the Equitable Life Insurance Assn. and the Mutual Life Insurance Co. of New York
Speaking to the toast, "The Federal Judiciary, the Highest Evidence of Our Ancestors' Wisdom" (Sons of the Revolution, St. Paul)
Address at the memorial services for Colonel Leonard A. Harris
Due Administration of Justice the Firmest Pillar of Good Government (delivered to the Union League Club of Chicago)
Address on the legal profession to the graduating class of the Law School of Maryland University
Remarks to the Kansas City Bar Association
Address on Kansas
Speaking to the toast, "Our Guest," at the reception of the Kansas City Bar honoring his appointment to the U.S. Circuit Court
Opening remarks to the U.S. district and circuit court in session in Nebraska
Opening remarks to the U.S. circuit court in session in Leavenworth
Remarks at reception honoring Judge Brewer at his home in Leavenworth
Opinion of the Court in the Brighton Ranch case
Instructions to the jury by Judge Brewer, Hillman case
Decision in the grievance case of the employees of the Denver and Rio Grande Railroad
Address to the Ladies of the Social Science Association in Leavenworth, probably delivered by David Josiah Brewer
Letters, published - In Scrapbook II (Box 7, Folder 228):
Conflict Between the Civil and Military, article contains Judge David J. Brewer's letter to Major General William T. Sherman
Ladies for School Directors, letter to the Editor of the Commercial
Our Relation to the Liquor Traffic, letter to the Editors, Palladium , initialed "B.," possibly D.J. Brewer
A Leaf from Africa, letter to the Editors, Palladium initialed "B.," possibly D.J.Brewer
The Venezuelan Boundary Dispute, letter to J. Frederick Green, Secretary International Arbitration and Peace Assn.
In Re Grandfather, letter to The Lawyer's Easy Chair , "Grandfather Browne," probably written by Justice Brewer
Telegrams of congratulations received by D.J. Brewer on his nomination to the U.S. Supreme Court
Telegram from President Benjamin Harrison notifying Brewer of his confirmation by the Senate (original)
Letter of congratulations from F.W. Lehmann
Letter of congratulations from H.J.Dennis
Judge Brewer's Resignation, article contains farewell letter from Brewer to Hon. A.H. Horton, Hon. H.M. Valentine (on the occasion his accepting the position of Circuit Judge)
A Woolsey Professorship, letter to the Editor of the College Courant suggesting a "Woolsey Professorship of International Law,"
Poems, published:
Setting Hens Against Their Will
Poem on George Washington
Writings of D.J.B. which appear in Scrapbook III and do not appear elsewhere in his papers:
Articles:
From the West Radiate the World's Controlling Forces
Interview with Justice Brewer, New York Times
Liberty and stability: Spirit of Individual Liberty Growing, Says Brewer; in the New York World
Additional interviews quoting Brewer at length:
Interview with Justice Brewer, New York American
Complete Text of Addresses - In Scrapbook III:
Address at Chautauqua, N.Y.
Address on the life of the nation, delivered to the graduating class of Rayen School, Youngstown, Ohio
Letters, published:
To the Editor of the News (Yale) regarding the future of Old South Middle
Appeal for the American Missionary Association
